edges.description: Orexin neuron degeneration produces decreased CSF orexin/hypocretin levels.
Loss of orexin signaling destabilizes wakefulness and causes excessive daytime sleepiness.
Loss of orexin signaling contributes to cataplexy in narcolepsy type 1.
Immune priming and T-cell receptor associations converge on cytotoxic T-cell targeting of HCRT neurons.
Cytotoxic T-cell targeting of HCRT neurons leads to selective orexin neuron loss.

nodes.description: Autoreactive CD8-positive T cells and cytotoxicity-related immune loci support a final effector step in which HCRT neurons are targeted and destroyed.

evidence.reference_title: The Role of T Cells in the Pathogenesis of Narcolepsy Type 1: A Narrative Review.
evidence.snippet: Studies have identified specific T cell subsets, including CD4+ and CD8+ T cells, that target HCRT neurons, contributing to their destruction.

evidence.reference_title: Narcolepsy risk loci outline role of T cell autoimmunity and infectious triggers in narcolepsy.
evidence.snippet: We fine-mapped GWAS signals within HLA (DQ0602, DQB1*03:01 and DPB1*04:02) and discovered seven novel associations (CD207, NAB1, IKZF4-ERBB3, CTSC, DENND1B, SIRPG, PRF1).

evidence.reference_title: Narcolepsy: an interface among neurology, immunology, sleep, and genetics.
evidence.snippet: Narcolepsy is a primary disorder of the central nervous system resulting from genetic, environmental, and immunological interactions defined as excessive daytime sleepiness plus cataplexy, hallucinations, sleep paralysis, and sleep fragmentation.

evidence.reference_title: TAK-861, a potent, orally available orexin receptor 2-selective agonist, produces wakefulness in monkeys and improves narcolepsy-like phenotypes in mouse models.
evidence.snippet: Narcolepsy type 1 (NT1) is associated with severe loss of orexin neurons and characterized by symptoms including excessive daytime sleepiness and cataplexy.

evidence.reference_title: High-resolution HLA sequencing and hypocretin receptor 2 autoantibodies in narcolepsy type 1 and type 2.
evidence.snippet: Narcolepsy is a sleep disorder caused by an apparent degeneration of orexin/hypocretin neurons in the lateral hypothalamic area and a subsequent decrease in orexin/hypocretin levels in the cerebrospinal fluid.

evidence.reference_title: Narcolepsy: an interface among neurology, immunology, sleep, and genetics.
evidence.snippet: Narcolepsy is a primary disorder of the central nervous system resulting from genetic, environmental, and immunological interactions defined as excessive daytime sleepiness plus cataplexy, hallucinations, sleep paralysis, and sleep fragmentation.

evidence.reference_title: TAK-861, a potent, orally available orexin receptor 2-selective agonist, produces wakefulness in monkeys and improves narcolepsy-like phenotypes in mouse models.
evidence.snippet: Narcolepsy type 1 (NT1) is associated with severe loss of orexin neurons and characterized by symptoms including excessive daytime sleepiness and cataplexy.

nodes.description: HLA-DQB1*06:02-associated immune susceptibility, T-cell receptor loci, and infectious or vaccine triggers support an antigen-specific immune mechanism that injures hypocretin neurons.

evidence.reference_title: The Role of T Cells in the Pathogenesis of Narcolepsy Type 1: A Narrative Review.
evidence.snippet: The strong association between narcolepsy and the HLA-DQB1*06:02 allele strongly indicates an autoimmune etiology for this condition. Increasing evidence suggests that T cells play a critical role in this autoimmune-mediated HCRT neuronal loss.

evidence.reference_title: Narcolepsy risk loci outline role of T cell autoimmunity and infectious triggers in narcolepsy.
evidence.snippet: Narcolepsy type 1 (NT1) is caused by a loss of hypocretin/orexin transmission. Risk factors include pandemic 2009 H1N1 influenza A infection and immunization with Pandemrix®.

evidence.reference_title: Narcolepsy risk loci outline role of T cell autoimmunity and infectious triggers in narcolepsy.
evidence.snippet: T cell receptor associations in NT1 modulated TRAJ*24, TRAJ*28 and TRBV*4-2 chain-usage. Partitioned heritability and immune cell enrichment analyses found genetic signals to be driven by dendritic and helper T cells.

nodes.description: Selective loss or dysfunction of hypocretin/orexin-secreting neurons in the lateral hypothalamus causes hypocretin deficiency and destabilizes sleep-wake and rapid eye movement sleep boundaries.

evidence.reference_title: High-resolution HLA sequencing and hypocretin receptor 2 autoantibodies in narcolepsy type 1 and type 2.
evidence.snippet: Narcolepsy is a sleep disorder caused by an apparent degeneration of orexin/hypocretin neurons in the lateral hypothalamic area and a subsequent decrease in orexin/hypocretin levels in the cerebrospinal fluid.

evidence.reference_title: The Role of T Cells in the Pathogenesis of Narcolepsy Type 1: A Narrative Review.
evidence.snippet: The etiology of NT1 is linked to the destruction of hypothalamic neurons responsible for the synthesis of the wake-promoting neuropeptide known as hypothalamic orexin.
